data.title: ASML could benefit if Trump-Xi meeting leads to lifting of chip equipment export ban: BofA
data.description:
ASML (ASML) could benefit if this month's meeting between President Trump and China's President Xi Jinping leads to a lifting of the semiconductor equipment export ban, Bank of America said.

“Given China’s ambition to build a domestic semiconductor industry and a local AI ecosystem; US/Dutch/Japan Semicap export controls towards China (high-end immersion tools are banned) preventing China from achieving their goal of manufacturing 5nm chips; and constrained rare earth materials (critical for military equipment) exports from China, we think that both parties have enough at stake to deliver a mutually positive outcome,” analyst Didier Scemama wrote in a note to clients.

“This could come in the form of relaxing Semicap export control rules against increasing supply of rare earth. Recall that in [October] last year, a phone call between Trump and Xi led to [a] higher supply of rare earth against a one-year moratorium on Semicap export ban towards affiliates of companies on the entities list (ultimately driving a major recovery in China in 2H25).” 

Scemama, who has a Buy rating and a €1,598 price target on ASML, said that any relaxation of export controls could result in other Wall Street firms raising estimates on ASML. ASML is the only company in the world that builds extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, which are crucial for making advanced semiconductors.

President Trump is set to meet with President Xi in Beijing later this month to discuss a wide range of topics, including trade. 

Separately, on Thursday, China released its economic growth plan for the next five years. As part of that, executives in the country's semiconductor industry have called for policies that would allow the country to essentially replicate ASML, Reuters reported.

data.title: Sable Offshore soars as DoJ opinion points to potential production restart
data.description:
Sable Offshore (SOC) +25.7% in Thursday's trading following a Department of Justice Office of Legal Counsel opinion that said presidential orders under the Defense Production Act can pre-empt state laws that conflict with federal energy production directives.

California state laws have prevented Sable (SOC) from operating the Santa Ynez offshore oil and gas facility in federal waters and transporting production through associated pipeline infrastructure.

Benchmark analyst Subash Chandra said in a note that bullish investors had been anticipating that the Trump administration would cite the Defense Production Act in backing production at Santa Ynez, but added the state of California would still seek to block the pipeline restart.

"It works really well if they were producing entirely offshore, but the fact that they have all these obstacles to get a hundred miles through California means it's going to be challenged," Chandra wrote in downgrading Sable (SOC) shares to Hold from Buy.

data.title: Oversold Chinese stocks: Alibaba leads low-RSI pack as Beijing targets 4.5-5% growth
data.description:
China’s decision to lower its 2026 GDP growth target to a 4.5%–5% range, the weakest official goal since the early 1990s, has underscored just how difficult the macro environment has become for its equity market.

Against that backdrop, many of China’s best-known internet and consumer-tech champions have continued to lag, leaving some of the market’s biggest names technically oversold.

That dynamic is visible in a screen of major large-cap Chinese stocks ranked by their Relative Strength Index, a momentum gauge that measures the speed and magnitude of recent price moves on a 0–100 scale. 

At the top of the list is Alibaba Group (BABA), with an RSI of 22, followed by Baidu (BIDU) at 24 and JD.com (JD) at 25, levels typically associated with heavily sold, out-of-favor names.

Tencent Holdings (TCEHY) and Tencent Music (TME) round out the top five with RSIs of 29 and 30, suggesting persistent selling pressure across China’s leading platform and entertainment companies.

Further down, DiDi (DIDIY), XPeng (XPEV), Xiaomi (XIACY), NetEase (NTES), Bilibili (BILI), and ZTO Express (ZTO) all sit below or around the 40 mark, still reflecting subdued momentum, while H World Group (HTHT) is the “least” oversold of the group with an RSI of 50.

In technical terms, RSI readings at or below 30 are often interpreted as potential oversold conditions that may precede a rebound if fundamentals stabilize, whereas levels above 70 are typically seen as overbought and vulnerable to pullbacks. Here, none of the names approach the overbought threshold

Below is the list:

  1. Alibaba Group (BABA), RSI: 22

  2. Baidu (BIDU), RSI: 24

  3. JD.com (JD), RSI: 25

  4. Tencent Holdings (TCEHY), RSI: 29

  5. Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), RSI: 30

  6. DiDi Global (DIDIY), RSI: 32

  7. XPeng (XPEV), RSI: 36

  8. Xiaomi (XIACY), RSI: 36

  9. NetEase (NTES), RSI: 37

  10. Bilibili (BILI), RSI: 38

  11. ZTO Express (ZTO), RSI: 39

  12. Baidu (BAIDF), RSI: 41

  13. Kuaishou Technology (KUASF), RSI: 41

  14. Li Auto (LI), RSI: 43

  15. Luckin Coffee (LKNCY), RSI: 44

  16. Yum China Holdings (YUMC), RSI: 46

  17. KE Holdings (BEKE), RSI: 46

  18. NIO (NIO), RSI: 48

  19. BYD Company (BYDDF), RSI: 49

  20. H World Group (HTHT), RSI: 50

China ETFs: (KWEB), (PGJ), (CQQQ), (FXI), (GXC), (MCHI), (FLCH), (CNYA), (ASHR), and (YINN)

data.title: Rise of robots? Texas Instruments joins forces with Nvidia to help developers build androids
data.description:
Texas Instruments (TXN) is combining its real-time control, sensing, and power portfolio tools with Nvidia's (NVDA) advanced robotics computing, ethernet-based sensing, and simulation technologies to help developers build and deploy humanoid robots.

"TI's comprehensive portfolio bridges the gap between NVIDIA's powerful AI compute and real-world applications, enabling developers to validate complete humanoid systems earlier in development," said Giovanni Campanella, general manager of industrial automation and robotics at Texas Instruments. "This integrated approach will help accelerate the evolution from prototypes to commercially viable humanoid robots operating safely alongside humans."

As part of this collaboration, TI designed a sensor fusion solution by integrating its mmWave radar technology with Nvidia Jetson Thor using Nvidia Holoscan Sensor Bridge to enable low-latency, 3D perception and safety awareness for humanoid robots. Texas Instruments plans to showcase the development during the upcoming Nvidia GTC event, which runs from March 16 through 19 in San Jose, Calif.

"The safe operation of humanoid robots in unpredictable environments requires a massive leap in processing power to synchronize complex AI models with real-time sensor data and motor controls," said Deepu Talla, vice president of robotics and edge AI at Nvidia.

By fusing camera and radar data, the solution improves object detection, localization, and tracking while reducing false positives for confident, real-time decision-making in humanoid robots, the companies said.

Several U.S.-based companies are working to develop humanoid robots. Tesla (TSLA) is busy developing Optimus, a humanoid bot being built for industrial and consumer uses. Figure AI, which is backed by Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI (OPENAI), is attempting to craft a general-purpose humanoid capable of handling all types of tasks.

"These robots can eliminate the need for unsafe and undesirable jobs—ultimately allowing us to live happier, more purposeful lives," Figure AI said.

Boston Dynamics is hoping its Atlas robot can "revolutionize industrial workspaces."

However, UC Berkeley professor and robotics expert Ken Goldberg, who also chairs the Berkeley AI Research Lab steering committee, contends in recent journal articles that engineers are years away from building humanoid robots with real-world skill levels.

"We're all very familiar with ChatGPT and all the amazing things it's doing for vision and language, but most researchers are very nervous about the analogy that most people have, which is that now that we've solved all these problems, we're ready to solve [humanoid robots], and it's going to happen next year," Goldberg said.  "I'm not saying it's not going to happen, but I'm saying it's not going to happen in the next two years, or five years, or even 10 years. We're just trying to reset expectations so that it doesn't create a bubble that could lead to a big backlash."

data.title: Brilliant Earth projects mid-single-digit net sales growth for 2026 amid fine jewelry expansion and margin headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Brilliant Earth Group, Inc. (BRLT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Beth Gerstein highlighted, "We delivered our largest quarter ever of net sales in Q4, driving 4% year-over-year growth in net sales with particularly strong performance in fine jewelry." Gerstein emphasized that fine jewelry now represents 17% of full-year bookings, stating, "2025 was a breakthrough year in which we grew fine jewelry mix to 17% of bookings with many iconic product releases."
  • The company reported net sales of $124.4 million for the fourth quarter, and $437.5 million for the full year. Gerstein noted strong holiday season results, with record-breaking cyber weekend sales and a 15% bookings growth in the 10 days leading up to Christmas.
  • Gross margin for Q4 was 55.9%, with Gerstein explaining, "Our ability to manage through these conditions has been a testament to the strength of our business model and our team."
  • The CEO announced the opening of the first flagship showroom in Beverly Hills and the plan to open two additional showrooms in 2026.
  • CFO Chuenhong Kuo stated, "Q4 net sales were $124.4 million, up 4.1% year-over-year, which was our biggest quarter ever and near the midpoint of our stated guidance range." Kuo added, "Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $4.2 million or a 3.3% margin and full year adjusted EBITDA of $12 million or a 2.7% margin, illustrating the agility of our business model and our continued discipline in cost management."

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 net sales to grow in the mid-single-digit percent range year-over-year.
  • Gross margin is expected to remain in the mid-50s percent range for 2026, assuming metal prices stay at current high levels.
  • Most of the adjusted EBITDA for 2026 is anticipated in Q4, with a warning that adjusted EBITDA for the year will be slightly lower than 2025 due to metal cost headwinds. Kuo specified, "We expect adjusted EBITDA margin to be in the negative mid-single-digit range as a percentage of sales" for Q1 2026.
  • Management withdrew medium-term targets due to volatility in metal prices, emphasizing a focus on annual guidance until visibility improves.

Financial Results

  • Full-year net sales reached $437.5 million, with Q4 net sales of $124.4 million.
  • Q4 adjusted EBITDA was $4.2 million, and full-year adjusted EBITDA was $12 million.
  • Total orders grew 6.5% year-over-year in Q4 and 13% for the full year. Repeat orders grew 15% in Q4.
  • Average order value was $2,001 in Q4 and $2,082 for the year, with fine jewelry bookings growing 34% year-over-year in Q4, reaching 23% of total bookings for the quarter.
  • Gross margin declined year-over-year, with Kuo noting, "Q4 gross margin was 55.9%, and full year gross margin was 57.5%. This represents a 370 basis point decline year-over-year in Q4 and a 280 basis point decline for the full year."
  • The company ended Q4 with $79.1 million in cash, no debt, and generated $5.8 million in free cash flow for the year.

Q&A

  • Julia Shelanski, TD Cowen: Asked about AOV growth expectations and metal hedging. Gerstein responded that ASPs are up due to brand strength and mix shift, but fine jewelry's lower ASP affects overall AOV. Kuo added, "We have a variety of different tools that we use. Hedging is one of those strategies."
  • Ashley Owens, KeyBanc: Inquired about 2026 bookings growth drivers and share gains. Gerstein indicated ongoing fine jewelry growth, showroom expansion, and brand awareness as key drivers and highlighted that fine jewelry now brings in half of new customers.
  • Owens followed up on fine jewelry's impact on business mix and margin outlook. Gerstein confirmed continued diversification beyond bridal, while Kuo addressed gross margin, "We do think that we have more tools over time... our agility overall that we've demonstrated in recent quarters continues to serve us well."
  • Anna Glaessgen, B. Riley: Asked about pricing actions in 2026 and operating expense opportunities. Gerstein said Q1 offers more opportunity for selective price increases due to less sensitivity than Q4, and Kuo pointed to ongoing marketing leverage and disciplined OpEx management, but cited metal prices as the main profitability headwind.
  • Glaessgen followed up on EBITDA seasonality, with Kuo confirming most 2026 profitability is expected in Q4 due to revenue seasonality and cost structure.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on margin headwinds, AOV trends, and sustainability of fine jewelry growth, with a neutral to slightly cautious tone, particularly about gross margin pressures and EBITDA seasonality.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing agility, but acknowledged external cost pressures and withdrew medium-term guidance. In Q&A, responses were measured, with frequent references to agility and mitigation tools.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from celebratory to more probing on margin sustainability, while management became more cautious regarding forward guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from multi-year targets to a singular focus on 2026 due to metal price volatility.
  • Fine jewelry’s role continues to expand, increasing from 14% to 17% of full-year bookings.
  • Gross margin declined from 57.6% in Q3 to 55.9% in Q4, with further pressure expected in 2026. Net sales growth moderated, and EBITDA margin guidance for 2026 is lower due to persistent cost headwinds.
  • Analysts now concentrate more on external risks and sustainability of margin improvements, a change from last quarter’s focus on top-line outperformance and engagement recovery.
  • Management’s tone is less assertive on long-term projections, reflecting uncertainty in the cost environment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified record-high metal prices and an unpredictable tariff environment as significant ongoing risks.
  • Mitigation strategies include dynamic pricing, hedging, supply chain optimization, and leveraging fine jewelry growth.
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned the sustainability of margin mitigation and the impact of metal price volatility on profitability and guidance.

Final Takeaway

Brilliant Earth concluded the quarter with record net sales, continued expansion in fine jewelry, and resilient profitability despite historic metal price headwinds. The company is focusing its outlook on 2026, projecting mid-single-digit net sales growth and continued marketing leverage, while preparing for ongoing gross margin pressure and a concentration of annual profitability in Q4. Management underscored its agility and disciplined expense management as key assets in navigating industry-wide challenges and sustaining long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: China reportedly expands restrictions on iron ore imports from BHP
data.description:
China's state-run iron ore buyer has widened restrictions on buying new seaborne iron ore cargoes from BHP (BHP), Reuters reported Thursday, as a months-long ​contract dispute drags on.

China Mineral Resources Group is said to have told several traders this week to buy fewer seaborne cargoes of BHP (BHP) Mac fines, Newman fines ​and Newman lumps, products that have not yet been part of the government's widely reported ​previous restrictions.

CMRG, set up in 2022 to centralize iron ‌ore ⁠buying and win better terms from miners, barred domestic steel mills and traders from buying BHP's (BHP) Jimblebar fines, a type of iron ore, in September, and extended the ban in November to Jinbao, another variety of iron ore.

CMRG and BHP (BHP) have been caught in a months-long standoff in talks for long-term contracts on behalf of China’s mills.

data.title: Iran conflict and impact on jet fuel likely to take a bigger bite out of American Airlines' profits
data.description:
The conflict in the Middle East and its immediate—and most likely material—impact on fuel prices have acute ramifications on operating costs for the airline sector. Although U.S. carriers have a limited exposure to flights in the region, the detrimental impact on costs led Rothschild-Redburn to downgrade American Airlines to Neutral from Buy to reflect the carrier’s heightened sensitivity to fuel prices.

Entering 2026, the outlook for the airline industry was generally favorable thanks to improving demand and “rational supply-side dynamics.” But with the Iran conflict added to the mix of increased domestic capacity, Rothschild-Redburn sees increased headwinds for the more vulnerable carriers.

“Given the correlation between jet fuel prices and EBIT margins, as U.S. airlines do not hedge jet fuel, further increases to oil prices from these levels add substantial pressure to our forecasts,” Rothschild-Redburn analyst James Goodall writes in his note to clients.

While fuel represents a relatively similar share of revenue and costs for each of the U.S. carriers, Goodall believes the diverging margin profiles mean there is a “substantial” difference in fuel as a percentage of EBIT. For example, in 2025, Goodall notes that fuel represented 1000% of Southwest’s (LUV) EBIT but just 170% for Delta (DAL).  

Using a model that assumes each $0.10 move in jet fuel per gallon results in a $0.30 to $0.40 impact to EPS, this makes American Airlines (AAL) the most sensitive to fuel prices and Delta (DAL) and United (UAL) less so.

“Clearly, the airlines will react to the higher jet fuel prices by attempting to pass through the higher costs to consumers to mitigate the impact; as such, we have increased our pricing assumptions this year,” Goodall adds. 

As far as how the Iran conflict will impact operations, United has the greatest exposure to the Middle East, with the area representing 4% of scheduled international available seat miles ((ASM)) followed by 2.5% for American (AAL) and 1.5% for Delta (DAL). Accordingly, Goodall says that with United (UAL) having the highest exposure to international supply, this leaves the carrier with the largest disruption risk and Delta (DAL) with the least. He maintains a Buy rating on United (UAL) and Delta (DAL) but lowers his target price by 12% and 3% to $110 and $70 for each, respectively.

Shares across the airline industry remain pressured by the conflict, with American (AAL), United (UAL), and Delta (DAL) all lower by 5% to 6% on Thursday.

data.title: CorMedix nears an 11-month low after earnings miss
data.description:
CorMedix (CRMD) shares lost ~12% on Thursday to reach the lowest level in nearly 11 months after the antimicrobial product developer fell well short of Street forecasts with its Q4 2025 earnings.

The maker of the DefenCath catheter lock solution reported $0.16 of earnings per share for the quarter, missing the consensus by $0.66 with a ~20% YoY decline as the company recorded $42.4M of tax expenses in Q4.

Its operating income rose more than 350% YoY to $59.1M, and revenue witnessed over a fourfold rise to $128.6M, exceeding the consensus by $1.6M. The company's Melinta portfolio added $37.4M during its first full quarter under CRMD after the acquisition closed in August.

For the full year, CorMedix (CRMD) recorded $311.7M of total revenue, including $258.8M in net sales from DefenCath and $52.9M in net revenue from the legacy Melinta portfolio. Meanwhile, its pro forma revenue for 2025, including full-year revenue from the Melinta portfolio, reached $401.3M.

Looking ahead, the company reaffirmed its outlook, indicating $300M - $320M in revenue for 2026 compared to $308.96M projected by analysts.

data.title: CrowdStrike in focus as Citi reiterates Buy rating after results
data.description:
CrowdStrike (CRWD) was in focus on Thursday as Citi reiterated its Buy rating on the cybersecurity company after it reported fiscal fourth-quarter results.

“Clean, above-median ARR/NNARR beats (0.6%/$29M vs. 0.5%/$19M median over L8Qs) with ~mid-20%s ARR/revs/NNARR outlook touch ahead of Street should reenergize stock momentum to sustain premium valuation, but myopically, the mild beat/guide quantum, YoY Cloud ARR deceleration, and muted profitability upside could rankle,” Fatima Boolani wrote in a note to clients. “This, however, misses the broader point: core endpoint continues to accelerate (2 consecutive Q; compares generally favorable), big wallets are being efficiently captured across multiple cyber-disciplines (Flex, meaty/high-moat distribution avenues prove entrenched incumbency and trust), and AI winning/monetizing is just hitting the S-curve (AIDR off to running-start; agentic still early with Charlotte AI intriguing proxy).”

Boolani added that the results and guidance were “respectable” and affirmed CrowdStrike's “enviable idiosyncratic demand/execution/leadership halos.” Despite reiterating the Buy rating on CrowdStrike, Boolani lowered her price target to $525 from $610 on a lower earnings multiple.

data.title: Waste Connections plans public offering of senior unsecured notes
data.description:
• Waste Connections (WCN:CA) announced plans to offer senior notes in an underwritten public offering.
  • The notes will be issued as senior unsecured obligations of the company.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds along with existing cash to repay part of its revolving credit facility borrowings.
  • The offering aims to optimize the company’s capital structure and manage existing debt obligations.
